title: From Pauli Strings to Quantum Dynamics: A Unified Characterization
authors: Roberto Gargiulo, Paul Herringer, Robert Zeier
abstract: Understanding the dynamical properties of quantum systems is an essential task in quantum computing, quantum control, and many-body physics. Tools such as representation theory and Lie theory provide crucial information on reachability and computational power. However, this information can be difficult to access exactly or compute efficiently for arbitrary generating sets. Here we focus on the setting of Pauli strings, which satisfy numerous exceptional properties that simplify the problem. We find deep connections between Pauli Lie algebras and certain subgroups of the Clifford group generated by transvections, through the symplectic properties of the Pauli strings. This allows us to give an invariant-based perspective on these objects and their reachability, in the language of Pauli orbits, symmetries, and invariant subspaces. The invariant-based approach provides efficient algorithms for identifying Lie algebras and orbits, as well as a simple framework for analyzing structured Pauli generating sets. We also show in an elementary way that Clifford subgroups generated by transvections provide 3-designs for the corresponding Pauli Lie groups. We illustrate the framework through structured examples from variational quantum algorithms, restricted quantum computation, many-body systems, and random circuits.
summary: This paper develops an invariant-based mathematical framework to characterize the dynamics generated by Pauli strings in quantum systems. By connecting Pauli Lie algebras to transvection groups using symplectic properties, it provides efficient algorithms to determine system symmetries and computational reachability. This unified approach is vital for analyzing structured quantum circuits used in variational algorithms and many-body physics.
main_problem: The core problem is understanding the dynamical properties, reachability, and computational power of quantum systems generated by sets of Pauli strings.
main_result: The authors establish a unified, invariant-based framework connecting Pauli Lie algebras to subgroups of the Clifford group via symplectic properties, allowing for efficient classification algorithms.
method: The methodology heavily relies on representation theory, Lie theory, and graph theory (specifically using frustration and commutator graphs) to analyze algebraic structures over $\mathbb{F}_2$.
model_or_system: The focus is on quantum dynamics generated by Pauli strings ($\{I, X, Y, Z\}^{\otimes n}$), which models restricted quantum computation, variational algorithms, and certain many-body interactions.
key_observables: Pauli orbits, invariant subspaces, the structure of the associated Lie algebra, and whether the generators provide 3-designs for the Pauli Lie group.
important_parameters: The system size $n$ (number of qubits) and the specific generating set of Paulis are key parameters determining the complexity and structure.
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to the setting where generators are Pauli strings, which simplifies the problem from general quantum dynamics to one solvable with polynomial-time algorithms based on invariants.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate graph formalisms, specifically comparing frustration graphs and commutator graphs for a given generating set of Paulis.
paper_structure: The paper is highly structured across multiple parts, moving from an overview to detailed framework development using Pauli strings, invariant theory, Clifford groups, and finally illustrating applications in VQAs and many-body systems.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful algebraic toolkit (invariant-based classification) to analyze the computational power of quantum circuits defined by local interactions, which is crucial for understanding resource limitations in quantum computation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The graph-theoretic formalism. Example of (a) frustration graph and (b) commutator graph over two qubits, for the generating set G = {IX, XI, ZZ}.

FIG. 2. Up to isomorphism, the partition of the system in three parts due to symmetries: m logical qubits, r phase qubits, ℓ uncontrollable qubits. Also, in orange, the three possible choices of reference invariant bilinear form, B = I, Ym, Xm+1. These invariant properties completely determine the quasi-universal cases.

FIG. 4. (a) Local Commutant of the Ising model/multi-angle QAOA and its directed graph Dcomm({XI, IX, ZZ}). (b) Lo- cal Invariant Bilinear Forms of the Ising model/multi-angle QAOA and its directed graph Dbil({XI, IX, ZZ}). There is a directed edge A →B whenever A and B locally match, i.e., they are of the form A = A1A2 and B = B1B2 with A2 = B1.
FIG. 3. Example of swap-symmetric 2-local ansatz on some connected graph H, with vertex operators Cu and edge oper- ators AuBv or AuAv.

FIG. 5. H is the interaction graph of a qubit system which interacts via an Ising interaction and subject to local transversal fields, as in the multi-angle QAOA setting. The terms Xu live on the vertices and ZuZv on the edges of H. Its frustration graph is the subdivision graph of the interaction graph.
